content.text: Every RAG pipeline starts with raw files — PDFs, Word documents, HTML pages, PowerPoint decks, and images. Parsing converts these source formats into clean Unicode text that can be chunked and embedded. The quality of parsing directly bounds retrieval quality: if the parser garbles a table, misorders columns, or drops a section, that information is permanently lost before it ever reaches the vector store. Parsing is the least glamorous but most foundational step in the ingestion pipeline.

Digitally-created PDFs embed a text layer that parsers can extract directly without OCR. PyPDF is the standard loader for this — it is fast, memory-efficient, and produces one Document per page. For PDFs with complex layouts (multi-column text, embedded tables, financial statements), pdfplumber gives better results because it uses coordinate-based extraction that preserves column order and can separately extract table cells. The choice between them is not permanent — use PyPDF for body-text documents and pdfplumber specifically for financial or tabular PDFs.

# Native PDF parsing: extract text from digitally-created PDFs
# PyPDF is fast and lightweight; pdfplumber gives better table and column extraction.

from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader
from langchain_core.documents import Document
import pdfplumber   # pip install pdfplumber

# --- PyPDF: one Document per page ---
loader = PyPDFLoader("docs/annual_report.pdf")
pages = loader.load()   # list[Document], one per page
print(f"Pages: {len(pages)}")
print(f"Page 1 metadata: {pages[0].metadata}")  # {source, page}
print(f"Page 1 text (first 200 chars): {pages[0].page_content[:200]}")

# --- pdfplumber: better for tables and multi-column layouts ---
def load_pdf_with_tables(pdf_path: str) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Extract text page by page; for pages with tables, extract them as
    tab-separated rows and append after the body text.
    """
    docs = []
    with pdfplumber.open(pdf_path) as pdf:
        for i, page in enumerate(pdf.pages):
            body = page.extract_text() or ""
            tables = page.extract_tables()
            table_text = ""
            for table in tables:
                for row in table:
                    if any(cell for cell in row if cell):
                        table_text += "\t".join(str(cell or "").strip() for cell in row) + "\n"
            combined = (body + "\n\n" + table_text).strip()
            if combined:
                docs.append(Document(
                    page_content=combined,
                    metadata={"source": pdf_path, "page": i + 1},
                ))
    return docs


docs = load_pdf_with_tables("docs/financial_statements.pdf")
print(f"Extracted {len(docs)} pages with text/tables")

Scanned PDFs are images — they have no embedded text layer. OCR (Optical Character Recognition) converts the pixel grid into Unicode text. Tesseract is the standard open-source engine: accurate on clean scans at 300 DPI, free, and runs entirely locally. For documents with complex layouts (forms, multi-column reports, mixed text and tables), AWS Textract produces better structure — it returns text blocks with spatial coordinates and separates form keys from values. Unstructured provides a unified interface that auto-detects whether a PDF needs OCR and handles the routing automatically.

# OCR for scanned documents: extract text from image-based PDFs and images
# Scanned PDFs contain images of text — no embedded text layer to extract.
# Tesseract is the standard open-source OCR engine; AWS Textract handles complex layouts.

import subprocess
from pathlib import Path
from langchain_core.documents import Document

# --- Tesseract via pytesseract: simple, free, runs locally ---
import pytesseract        # pip install pytesseract
from PIL import Image     # pip install Pillow
import fitz               # pip install PyMuPDF — for PDF-to-image conversion

def ocr_pdf_with_tesseract(pdf_path: str, dpi: int = 300) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Convert each PDF page to a high-res image, then run Tesseract OCR.
    300 DPI is the sweet spot: below 200 DPI causes character recognition errors.
    """
    docs = []
    pdf = fitz.open(pdf_path)
    for i, page in enumerate(pdf):
        mat = fitz.Matrix(dpi / 72, dpi / 72)   # scale to target DPI
        pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=mat)
        img = Image.frombytes("RGB", [pix.width, pix.height], pix.samples)
        text = pytesseract.image_to_string(img, config="--psm 3")  # auto page segmentation
        if text.strip():
            docs.append(Document(
                page_content=text,
                metadata={"source": pdf_path, "page": i + 1, "ocr_engine": "tesseract"},
            ))
    return docs


# --- AWS Textract: handles forms, tables, and multi-column layouts ---
import boto3
import json

def ocr_with_textract(image_bytes: bytes) -> str:
    """
    Use AWS Textract to extract text from a document image.
    Textract preserves reading order and handles complex layouts better than Tesseract.
    """
    client = boto3.client("textract", region_name="us-east-1")
    response = client.detect_document_text(
        Document={"Bytes": image_bytes}
    )
    lines = [
        block["Text"]
        for block in response["Blocks"]
        if block["BlockType"] == "LINE"
    ]
    return "\n".join(lines)


# --- Unstructured: unified interface for PDFs, images, DOCX, PPTX, HTML ---
# Automatically detects whether a PDF needs OCR or can be extracted natively.
from unstructured.partition.auto import partition

elements = partition("docs/mixed_content.pdf")  # auto-selects OCR if needed
text = "\n\n".join(str(e) for e in elements if str(e).strip())
print(f"Extracted {len(elements)} structural elements via Unstructured")

Word documents carry structural metadata that plain-text extraction discards. Using python-docx to walk paragraphs and split on heading styles produces section-aligned documents — chunks that start and end at logical boundaries, not arbitrary character counts. For HTML, the main challenge is separating content from chrome: navigation bars, footers, sidebars, and cookie banners constitute 30–60% of a typical web page's text but contain no information relevant to retrieval. trafilatura is a specialized content extractor that uses heuristics to identify the main article body and discard the rest.

# Parsing DOCX and HTML: structure-aware extraction
# DOCX preserves headings, tables, and lists as distinct elements.
# HTML parsing should remove navigation, ads, and boilerplate before extracting body text.

from langchain_community.document_loaders import Docx2txtLoader, BSHTMLLoader
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from docx import Document as DocxDocument   # pip install python-docx
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup               # pip install beautifulsoup4
import trafilatura                          # pip install trafilatura — better web content extraction

# --- DOCX: preserve heading hierarchy ---
def load_docx_with_structure(path: str) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Split DOCX into sections by heading. Each section becomes a Document
    so chunk boundaries align with logical document structure.
    """
    docx = DocxDocument(path)
    sections = []
    current_heading = "Introduction"
    current_text = []

    for para in docx.paragraphs:
        if para.style.name.startswith("Heading"):
            if current_text:
                sections.append(Document(
                    page_content="\n".join(current_text),
                    metadata={"source": path, "section": current_heading},
                ))
            current_heading = para.text.strip()
            current_text = []
        else:
            if para.text.strip():
                current_text.append(para.text.strip())

    if current_text:
        sections.append(Document(
            page_content="\n".join(current_text),
            metadata={"source": path, "section": current_heading},
        ))
    return sections


# --- HTML: trafilatura for web pages (removes nav, ads, footers) ---
def load_html_clean(html_content: str, url: str) -> Document | None:
    """
    trafilatura extracts the main article body from HTML.
    Falls back to BeautifulSoup paragraph extraction if trafilatura returns nothing.
    """
    # Try trafilatura first
    text = trafilatura.extract(
        html_content,
        include_tables=True,
        include_links=False,
        no_fallback=False,
    )
    if text and len(text.strip()) > 100:
        return Document(page_content=text.strip(), metadata={"source": url})

    # Fallback: BeautifulSoup paragraph join
    soup = BeautifulSoup(html_content, "html.parser")
    for tag in soup(["nav", "header", "footer", "script", "style", "aside"]):
        tag.decompose()
    paragraphs = [p.get_text(separator=" ", strip=True) for p in soup.find_all("p")]
    body = "\n\n".join(p for p in paragraphs if len(p) > 30)
    if body:
        return Document(page_content=body, metadata={"source": url})
    return None


import requests
resp = requests.get("https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retrieval-augmented_generation", timeout=10)
doc = load_html_clean(resp.text, resp.url)
print(f"Extracted {len(doc.page_content)} chars from HTML")
Column merging: multi-column PDFs read left-to-right across all columns, mixing text from column 1 row 2 with column 2 row 1. Use pdfplumber's column-detection or Unstructured to fix this.
Password-protected PDFs: PyPDF raises an exception silently in some versions — always test the extracted text length, not just the absence of an exception.
OCR at low DPI: Tesseract accuracy degrades sharply below 200 DPI. Convert PDFs at 300 DPI minimum.
Encoding errors: older PDFs and Windows DOCX files sometimes use Windows-1252 encoding. Always decode to UTF-8 with errors='replace' and then strip replacement characters.
Empty extraction: a page that returns empty string from PyPDF is almost always a scanned image — detect this and route to the OCR path automatically rather than silently dropping the content.

Real corpora contain mixed file types. A routing layer that selects the correct parser based on file extension — and additionally detects whether a PDF is scanned or native — eliminates the most common parsing failure: using PyPDF on a scanned document and getting silently empty pages. The is_scanned_pdf heuristic (check whether the first few pages have meaningful text) is fast and reliable for the vast majority of documents. Route to OCR only when necessary; OCR is 10–50× slower than native extraction.

# Automatic format routing: detect file type and select the correct parser
# A single entry point that routes each file to its parser prevents the "wrong parser" bug.

from pathlib import Path
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader, TextLoader, BSHTMLLoader
import pytesseract
import fitz              # PyMuPDF for PDF-to-image conversion
from PIL import Image
from docx import Document as DocxDocument


def is_scanned_pdf(pdf_path: str, sample_pages: int = 3) -> bool:
    """
    Detect if a PDF needs OCR: check whether the first N pages have meaningful text.
    A page with < 50 characters is almost certainly a scanned image.
    """
    loader = PyPDFLoader(pdf_path)
    pages = loader.load()[:sample_pages]
    text_pages = [p for p in pages if len(p.page_content.strip()) >= 50]
    return len(text_pages) == 0


def ocr_pdf(pdf_path: str, dpi: int = 300) -> list[Document]:
    pdf = fitz.open(pdf_path)
    docs = []
    for i, page in enumerate(pdf):
        mat = fitz.Matrix(dpi / 72, dpi / 72)
        pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=mat)
        img = Image.frombytes("RGB", [pix.width, pix.height], pix.samples)
        text = pytesseract.image_to_string(img, config="--psm 3")
        if text.strip():
            docs.append(Document(
                page_content=text,
                metadata={"source": pdf_path, "page": i + 1, "parser": "tesseract"},
            ))
    return docs


def parse_docx(path: str) -> list[Document]:
    docx = DocxDocument(path)
    paragraphs = [p.text.strip() for p in docx.paragraphs if p.text.strip()]
    return [Document(page_content="\n".join(paragraphs), metadata={"source": path, "parser": "docx"})]


EXTENSION_ROUTER = {
    ".txt": lambda p: TextLoader(p).load(),
    ".md":  lambda p: TextLoader(p).load(),
    ".html": lambda p: BSHTMLLoader(p).load(),
    ".htm":  lambda p: BSHTMLLoader(p).load(),
    ".docx": parse_docx,
}


def parse_file(file_path: str) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Route to the correct parser based on file extension.
    PDFs are auto-detected for scanned vs native content.
    """
    ext = Path(file_path).suffix.lower()

    if ext == ".pdf":
        if is_scanned_pdf(file_path):
            return ocr_pdf(file_path)
        else:
            return PyPDFLoader(file_path).load()

    handler = EXTENSION_ROUTER.get(ext)
    if handler is None:
        print(f"Unsupported format: {file_path} — skipping")
        return []
    return handler(file_path)


# Batch parse a directory
import glob
all_docs = []
for path in glob.glob("docs/**/*.*", recursive=True):
    docs = parse_file(path)
    all_docs.extend(docs)
    if docs:
        print(f"Parsed {path}: {len(docs)} docs, {sum(len(d.page_content) for d in docs)} chars")

print(f"Total: {len(all_docs)} documents from {len(set(d.metadata['source'] for d in all_docs))} files")

Each parser produces slightly different text artifacts. PDF extraction creates ligature characters (fi, fl rendered as single Unicode codepoints), OCR introduces spurious hyphens and garbled accented characters, and DOCX files use smart quotes and em-dashes. A single normalization pass applied after parsing and before chunking handles all of these uniformly: NFC Unicode normalization, ligature replacement, control character stripping, and whitespace collapsing. Running it once here means chunking and embedding code can assume clean UTF-8 input.

# Post-parsing normalization: clean extracted text before chunking
# Raw parsed text has noise specific to each format: PDF ligatures, DOCX smart quotes,
# OCR artifacts, and HTML entity escapes. Normalize all of them in one pass.

import re
import unicodedata
from langchain_core.documents import Document


def normalize_text(text: str) -> str:
    """
    Apply format-agnostic normalization. Safe to run on all source formats.
    Order matters: encode→ligatures→whitespace→control chars→dedent.
    """
    # 1. Normalize Unicode to NFC (combines composed forms, fixes ligature encoding)
    text = unicodedata.normalize("NFC", text)

    # 2. Replace common OCR/PDF ligature artifacts with ASCII equivalents
    ligatures = {
        "\ufb01": "fi", "\ufb02": "fl", "\ufb03": "ffi", "\ufb04": "ffl",
        "\u2019": "'", "\u2018": "'", "\u201c": '"''", "\u201d": '"''",
        "\u2013": "-", "\u2014": "--", "\u2026": "...",
    }
    for char, replacement in ligatures.items():
        text = text.replace(char, replacement)

    # 3. Strip non-printable control characters (except newline, tab)
    text = re.sub(r"[^\x09\x0A\x20-\x7E\u00A0-\uFFFF]", "", text)

    # 4. Normalize whitespace: collapse multiple spaces, trim lines
    lines = [line.strip() for line in text.splitlines()]
    text = re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", "\n".join(lines))

    return text.strip()


def normalize_documents(docs: list[Document]) -> list[Document]:
    """Normalize all documents; drop any that are too short after normalization."""
    normalized = []
    for doc in docs:
        clean = normalize_text(doc.page_content)
        if len(clean) >= 100:  # drop pages/sections with almost no content
            normalized.append(Document(page_content=clean, metadata=doc.metadata))
    print(f"After normalization: {len(normalized)}/{len(docs)} documents retained")
    return normalized


# Usage: normalize immediately after parsing
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader

raw_pages = PyPDFLoader("docs/legal_contract.pdf").load()
clean_pages = normalize_documents(raw_pages)
total_chars = sum(len(d.page_content) for d in clean_pages)
print(f"Total clean chars: {total_chars}")

Document parsing is the first and most fragile step in the RAG ingestion pipeline. The output quality is bounded by parsing quality — garbled text cannot be recovered by better chunking or a larger embedding model downstream. Select the parser by format: PyPDF for digital PDFs, Tesseract or Textract for scanned documents, python-docx for Word files with structural headings, and trafilatura for web pages. Detect parsing failures by checking extracted text length, and route automatically to OCR when a PDF page returns empty text.
Empty text after extraction almost always means a scanned PDF — route to OCR.
pdfplumber outperforms PyPDF for tables and multi-column layouts.
Unstructured handles auto-detection across all formats — use it for heterogeneous corpora.
trafilatura removes web page boilerplate more reliably than BeautifulSoup paragraph joins.
